region_of_origin: China (Beijing origin; widely consumed in Singapore and across Chinese diaspora)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Zha Jiang Mian in Singapore is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of wheat noodles topped with a generous portion of minced pork soybean paste sauce and fresh vegetable garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Zha Jiang Mian — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Zha Jiang Mian (CR ID F05060137). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork mince and soybean paste composition as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Zha Jiang Mian in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the closest analogous food: wheat noodles (boiled, Chinese-style) with GI approximately 47–53 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The fermented soybean paste sauce and dietary fibre (3.2 g/100g) are expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (50) × available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 16.8 g/100g = 58.8 g) / 100 = 29.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 304 mg/100g from fermented soybean paste and sweet bean sauce; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,064 mg sodium (46% of the 2,300 mg daily limit).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Zha Jiang Mian to a half-portion (≤175 g) or request reduced soybean paste to keep per-meal sodium below 600 mg, and balance the remainder of the day's intake with low-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 16.8 g/100g with dietary fibre 3.2 g/100g; estimated GI ~50 (low-medium boundary); a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 58.8 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~29.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include Zha Jiang Mian in a meal plan by limiting the serving to 200–250 g, choosing whole-wheat noodles where available to further reduce glycaemic response, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at ~1.9 g/100g from minced pork; sodium 304 mg/100g; dietary fibre 3.2 g/100g provides partial cardiovascular benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose a smaller portion (≤200 g), request leaner pork or substitute with minced chicken or tofu to reduce saturated fat intake, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to support LDL reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced pork (red meat) contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; sodium 304 mg/100g may exacerbate tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute minced pork with minced chicken or firm tofu, increase vegetable toppings (cucumber, carrot, edamame) for antioxidant support, and request reduced soybean paste to lower sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zha Jiang Mian (炸酱面, literally 'fried sauce noodles') is a classic Chinese noodle dish originating from Beijing and widely popular across the Chinese diaspora in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of thick or thin wheat noodles topped with a rich, savoury sauce made by stir-frying minced pork with fermented soybean paste (黄豆酱) and sweet bean sauce (甜面酱), often seasoned with garlic, ginger, and a small amount of sugar. The sauce is dark brown, umami-forward, slightly sweet and salty, with a thick, clingy texture that coats the noodles. Fresh garnishes such as julienned cucumber, shredded carrot, blanched bean sprouts, and spring onion are typically served alongside or on top, adding crunch and freshness. Per 100 g, the dish provides 145 kcal, 5.7 g protein, 6.15 g fat, 16.8 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 304 mg sodium. The dietary fibre content is notable for a noodle dish and comes from the soybean paste and vegetable garnishes. The sodium level is moderate-to-high due to the fermented paste base, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 50 (low-medium boundary), and a standard 350 g serving carries a glycaemic load of approximately 29, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content, and contains gluten (wheat) and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060137) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork mince and soybean paste composition. GI estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (boiled wheat noodles, GI ~47–53). Cultural metadata corrected from erroneous 'western' classification to 'Chinese'.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.9 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for minced pork (approximately 30% of total fat) and soybean paste composition, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. Input cultural metadata incorrectly classified this dish as 'western' with 'plant_based' dietary tag; corrected to Chinese cuisine with accurate dietary flags (contains pork, not plant-based). 4. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on boiled Chinese wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Zha Jiang Mian exists in approved sources. 5. Halal status is false due to pork content. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
